meta.title: Defense Showcase | Web-first Presentation
meta.description: A stage-driven defense presentation rebuilt as an offline-ready web deck.

hero.title: A Stage-first Web Defense
hero.subtitle: Instead of copying slides into a browser, this deck reorganizes the narrative into a clearer, more rhythmic, and more presentation-friendly structure.

overview.title: Structure Before Argument
overview.subtitle: The whole deck moves through motivation, problem statement, theorem, proof path, experiments, and contribution so the narrative remains coherent in a live defense.

cards.title: Background
cards.body: Explain why the research object matters and where current methods still fall short.

cards.title: Question
cards.body: State the mathematical question precisely and define the scope of the work.

cards.title: Theorem
cards.body: Present the main result early so the audience understands the payoff.

cards.title: Evidence
cards.body: Use experiments and summary statements to make the result credible and reusable.

bullets: Each page carries one narrative objective.
The route from problem to conclusion remains traceable.
A unified visual system makes the defense easier to deliver live.

problem.title: Why This Problem Matters
problem.subtitle: A strong defense does not begin with formulas. It begins by making the stakes and the difficulty visible.
points: Existing methods still have gaps in convergence, stability, or applicability.
Without a stronger analytical frame, the theory and the numerics remain disconnected.
This work aims to provide a more robust path instead of a local technical patch.

theorem.title: The Main Theorem
theorem.subtitle: Organize the theory through assumptions, conclusion, and proof path so the result stays precise and readable.
theorem.theorem: Theorem 1: Under assumptions H1-H3, the system admits a unique solution together with a stable convergence estimate and an explicit error bound.
assumptions: H1: Initial and boundary data satisfy the required regularity.
H2: The nonlinear term satisfies a local or global Lipschitz condition.
H3: The discrete scheme remains uniformly stable with the continuous model.
takeaways: Theoretical bounds match the experimental trend.
The result covers a broader parameter regime.
The proof pattern can transfer to adjacent models.

method.title: The Proof Path
method.subtitle: The goal here is not to show every detail. It is to make the route toward the theorem legible.

steps.title: Set the frame
steps.body: Fix notation, function spaces, and error measures so later arguments share the same language.

steps.title: Build estimates
steps.body: Construct the key a priori estimates and suppress the unstable terms step by step.

steps.title: Close the proof
steps.body: Use continuity or compactness arguments to complete existence and uniqueness.

steps.title: Link numerics
steps.body: Connect the theoretical bound to the observed experimental behavior.

results.title: Results That Support the Theory
results.subtitle: Parameter comparisons, error decay, and trend alignment are used to verify that the method behaves as predicted.
bullets: Key indicators show a stable convergence trend as the step size decreases.
Different parameter groups behave consistently with the theoretical analysis.
The numerical outcomes show that the method is practically usable.
sideNotes: Error slope matches the expected order

contributions.title: What This Work Contributes
contributions.subtitle: Compress the contribution into theory, method, and presentation so the committee can form a sharp overall judgment.

cards.title: Theory
cards.body: The work clarifies convergence and stability where earlier accounts remained weak.

cards.title: Method
cards.body: It leaves behind a reusable proof route that can be adapted to related models.

cards.title: Delivery
cards.body: It also turns the defense itself into a stage-ready web experience.

closing.title: Thank you for your attention
closing.subtitle: ECUST Mathematics Defense ? Web-first Showcase
closing.quote: If the conclusion is credible, the path is clear, and the value is explicit, the defense is already standing on solid ground.
footer: Questions on the model, theorem, and experiments are welcome.
